summary: In a 2004 Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting excerpt, Warren Buffett emphasizes that the core principles of analyzing securities remain unchanged over time. He argues that emotional temperament and self-control are far more critical to investing success than raw intellect, warning that a lack of discipline inevitably leads to disaster.

principles.statement: The fundamentals of analyzing securities do not change over time.
principles.rationale: Despite market evolution and technological advances, the core principles of value investing remain exactly the same as they were decades ago.
principles.source_quote: There’s nothing different, in my view, about analyzing securities now than there was 50 years ago

principles.statement: Temperament is far more important than intellect in investing.
principles.rationale: Without emotional discipline and self-control, even highly intelligent people will make irrational decisions and experience disasters in the market.
principles.source_quote: The temperament is all important. I mean, if you can’t control yourself, no matter what the intellect you bring to the process, you’re going to have disasters.

principles.statement: Invest in businesses where intrinsic value far exceeds the price.
principles.rationale: A patient, analytical mindset should be used to identify obvious discrepancies between a company's true value and its market price.
principles.source_quote: We would look for things that jumped out at us as being very cheap in relation to the value

mental_models.name: Business Mind vs. Money Mind
mental_models.description: A distinction highlighting that success in investing requires a specific 'business mind' rather than just a general affinity for money. Two people with identical IQs might be naturally adapted to one mindset over the other.

heuristics: Look for things that jump out at you as being very cheap in relation to their value.

signature_quotes.text: There’s nothing different, in my view, about analyzing securities now than there was 50 years ago
signature_quotes.context: Speaking on how the core principles of value investing remain unchanged despite technological advances.

signature_quotes.text: The temperament is all important. I mean, if you can’t control yourself, no matter what the intellect you bring to the process, you’re going to have disasters.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why emotional discipline matters more than IQ in investing.

signature_quotes.text: What we learn from history is that people don’t learn from history.
signature_quotes.context: Commenting on how investors repeatedly get caught up in speculative bubbles and market manias.

signature_quotes.text: We would look for things that jumped out at us as being very cheap in relation to the value
signature_quotes.context: Describing his and Charlie Munger's disciplined approach to finding investments.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on high intellect while lacking emotional discipline.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Without self-control, investors abandon rational thinking during speculative bubbles and market euphoria, leading to financial disasters regardless of their IQ.

anti_patterns.description: Ignoring the lessons of past market cycles.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Investors repeatedly fall into the same traps of market manias and speculative bubbles because they fail to learn from history.
